gaps.label: Work redesign
gaps.description: How deeply AI is embedded into end-to-end workflow redesign, ownership, and scaling discipline.

gaps.label: Governance
gaps.description: How clearly autonomy boundaries, accountability, and operational controls are defined and enforced.

gaps.label: Value measurement
gaps.description: How consistently AI initiatives are linked to measurable outcomes, multi-dimensional value, and executive reporting.

questions.prompt: Each priority AI workflow has a named business owner accountable for end-to-end outcomes.
Decision rights between frontline teams, functional leaders, and central AI support are explicit for redesigned workflows.
Cross-functional workflows using AI are governed by a shared operating model instead of siloed local practices.
Role definitions and handoffs are formally redesigned when AI changes how work is executed.
Baseline metrics are captured before AI deployment for each target workflow.
Workflow performance is measured using end-to-end cycle time and quality metrics, not only activity or usage counts.
Exception and rework patterns are reviewed regularly to improve redesigned workflows.
Performance metrics trigger concrete workflow or adoption actions when targets are missed.
AI pilots are scoped to complete business workflows rather than isolated task demonstrations.
Go/no-go criteria for pilots are defined before execution and used in final decisions.
Each pilot has an assigned owner and plan for production transition if outcomes are proven.
Pilot lessons are codified and reused to accelerate adjacent workflow redesign.
Autonomy levels are explicitly defined (for example, assisted, supervised, audited) for AI-enabled workflows.
Allowed AI actions are mapped to risk tiers with clear boundaries for autonomous execution.
Escalation rules are consistently applied when AI outputs fall outside approved autonomy boundaries.
Autonomy is expanded only after evidence demonstrates reliability, reversibility, and control.
A named owner is accountable for outcomes when AI-assisted decisions impact customers, operations, or compliance.
RACI-style accountability is documented across business, data, and technology roles for each high-impact AI workflow.
Governance policies are operationally enforced through approvals, controls, and audit evidence rather than documentation alone.
Incident response playbooks define accountability, rollback, and communication for AI failures.
Decision logs capture key model outputs, human overrides, and rationale for high-impact AI actions.
Monitoring covers model performance drift, data quality, and operational anomalies with active alerts.
User and stakeholder feedback is integrated into a closed-loop process for model and workflow improvement.
Guardrails and governance controls are reviewed on a defined cadence using monitoring evidence.
Outcome hypotheses are defined before deployment for each AI initiative.
Hypotheses focus on workflow outcomes (such as cycle time, quality, and decision speed) rather than tool adoption alone.
Each hypothesis includes an accountable owner, target timeframe, and measurable success threshold.
Hypotheses are recalibrated based on pilot and production evidence rather than remaining static.
Value tracking combines efficiency, quality, and capacity recovery metrics for major AI workflows.
Measurement captures new capabilities enabled by AI, not only cost reduction.
A standardized portfolio view compares AI initiatives using consistent value dimensions.
Tradeoffs between speed, quality, risk, and cost are made explicit in value reporting.
AI value is reported to executive or board stakeholders on a regular cadence.
Board-level reporting links AI investment to measured outcomes and strategic priorities.
Executive reporting includes governance maturity and risk signal alongside value performance.
Board and executive discussions directly inform next-quarter AI investment and scaling priorities.
